title: Input Validation & Security
beginnerExplanation: Real web apps need to collect, validate, and store user input from HTML forms. This involves handling POST requests in Flask, sanitizing data to prevent security issues, and persisting it to a database. Python's string methods, regular expressions, and database libraries make this manageable.
intermediateExplanation: Every useful app takes input: login forms, search bars, settings pages. Unvalidated input causes crashes, security holes, and data corruption. Learning to handle input properly is the difference between a toy project and a production application.

realWorldAnalogy.title: Airport Security Checkpoint
realWorldAnalogy.description: User input is like passengers at an airport. You cannot let everyone through unchecked. Security (validation) examines each passenger (input), rejects prohibited items (malicious data), and only lets clean passengers through to the gate (your database).

mentalModel: User input is like passengers at an airport. You cannot let everyone through unchecked. Security (validation) examines each passenger (input), rejects prohibited items (malicious data), and only lets clean passengers through to the gate (your database).

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Directly embedding user input into SQL queries — this causes SQL injection vulnerabilities.
Not checking for empty or missing form fields.
Trusting client-side validation alone — always validate on the server too.
Forgetting to set methods=["POST"] on routes that handle form submissions.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Input Validation & Security but hit a common pitfall: Directly embedding user input into SQL queries — this causes SQL injection vulnerabilities.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Always validate and sanitize user input — never trust it blindly.

practiceQuestions.question: Why should you never embed user input directly into SQL queries?
practiceQuestions.explanation: SQL injection allows attackers to execute arbitrary SQL commands by crafting malicious input. Always use parameterized queries.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Input Validation & Security?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

projectExercise.title: Mastering Input Validation & Security
projectExercise.description: Implement a clean snippet demonstrating the use of Input Validation & Security.

bossBattle.name: The Injection Serpent
bossBattle.description: A cunning serpent that slithers through your input fields, injecting malicious SQL commands. You must build impenetrable input validation to seal every entry point.
bossBattle.mechanic: Fix vulnerable code that is susceptible to SQL injection and XSS attacks by implementing proper sanitization.

artifacts.name: The Sanitizer Gauntlet
artifacts.description: A gauntlet that purifies any input, stripping away malicious characters.

This involves handling POST requests in Flask, sanitizing data to prevent security issues, and persisting it to a database. developerMode.industryUsage: Input Validation & Security is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Always validate and sanitize user input — never trust it blindly.
Use .strip() to remove whitespace, .lower() for case-insensitive matching.
Set reasonable length limits on text inputs to prevent abuse.
Use parameterized queries for database operations to prevent SQL injection.
antiPatterns: Directly embedding user input into SQL queries — this causes SQL injection vulnerabilities.
Not checking for empty or missing form fields.
Trusting client-side validation alone — always validate on the server too.
Forgetting to set methods=["POST"] on routes that handle form submissions.
